structured_choices.data_source: Electronic health records from a regional hospital system spanning 36 months of patient encounters. Source: production EHR system with complete audit trails. Data includes all inpatient discharge records with documented 30-day follow-up status.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: readmission within 30 days of discharge (index discharge). Label is derived from documented inpatient admission records within 30 calendar days post-discharge. Any admission for any reason qualifies as readmission. Readmission status is deterministic and locked at day 31 post-discharge; no labels are assigned to patients with incomplete follow-up windows at data freeze date.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with stratification by discharge reason category. Months 1–24 for training (n₁), months 25–30 for validation (n₂), months 31–36 for test (n₃). Justification: (1) Temporal ordering prevents label leakage — the model cannot access future readmission information when predicting past discharges; (2) Hospital practices, patient populations, and treatment protocols may drift over 36 months, so temporal splits reflect deployment reality; (3) Stratification by discharge reason ensures each split contains similar case-mix composition, preventing distribution shift. No randomization is used because patient trajectory data is inherently sequential; shuffling would destroy temporal dependencies the graph neural network is designed to exploit.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scalers, encoders, imputation models) are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–24). The fitted transformers are applied identically to validation and test sets. Temporal features (e.g., days since prior admission, treatment intensity trends) are computed per patient using only data available up to and including the index discharge date — never future data. This prevents temporal leakage.
structured_choices.features: Raw signal: all diagnosis codes (ICD-10), procedure codes (CPT), medication records, lab results with timestamps, vital signs, and clinical notes from index admission and complete patient history up to discharge. Constructed features: (1) Node features per temporal graph: patient demographics (age, sex, insurance type), comorbidity burden (Elixhauser index computed from diagnosis history), admission acuity (severity of illness score), length of stay, discharge disposition; (2) Edge features: time intervals between prior admissions, treatment transitions (e.g., medication changes), lab value trajectories (trend direction and volatility over preceding 12 months); (3) Temporal graph structure: nodes represent clinical events (admissions, labs, procedures), edges represent causal or temporal proximity (same admission, sequential procedures); (4) Confound features held in both models: socioeconomic status proxy (insurance type, zip-code-level median income), comorbidity count, age group. No features derived from 30-day post-discharge data. All temporal features are computed using information available at discharge time.
structured_choices.model: Temporal Graph Neural Network (TGNN): a graph attention network with temporal encoding of edges and node features. Architecture: (1) Nodes initialized with patient-level static features and temporal aggregations of past events; (2) Temporal convolutions over patient event sequences up to discharge; (3) Multi-head attention to weight relationships between clinical events; (4) Output layer: binary classification with logistic output. Hyperparameters tuned on validation set (see model_selection_data). Implementation: PyTorch Geometric or similar library with reproducible random seed.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression with the same constructed feature set as TGNN, fit via sklearn with L2 regularization (C parameter tuned via grid search on validation data). Baseline receives: all static features (demographics, comorbidities), summary statistics of temporal sequences (mean, max, min, std of labs; count of prior admissions; time since last admission), and confound features. Baseline does NOT receive explicit graph structure or temporal convolutions — it treats features as independent vectors. Tuning effort: baseline receives equivalent hyperparameter search (L2 penalty via 5-fold cross-validation on training set) as TGNN receives for its architecture and learning rate. Both models are evaluated on the same validation and test sets under identical conditions. This ensures the comparison isolates the value of temporal graph structure, not differences in tuning effort.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC). Justification: (1) Readmission prediction is inherently a ranking problem — the hospital needs to identify high-risk patients for early intervention; (2) AUROC is threshold-agnostic and does not require pre-specifying a decision threshold, which is appropriate when threshold choice depends on operational capacity (bed availability, intervention cost); (3) Readmission rates are typically moderate (10–25% depending on population), so AUROC is stable and interpretable; (4) Precision-Recall curve (AUPRC) is secondary to AUROC here because the primary stakeholder need is to rank patients by risk, not to optimize for a single operating point. AUROC directly aligns with the hypothesis — if TGNN better captures temporal patient dynamics, it should rank high-risk patients higher.
secondary_metrics: Sensitivity (recall) at fixed specificity thresholds (0.85, 0.90) — operationally relevant if the hospital has a fixed intervention budget
Positive Predictive Value (PPV) — for communication with clinicians about confidence in flagged readmission-risk patients
Calibration: Brier score and calibration plots — ensures predicted probabilities reflect true readmission risk, critical for resource allocation
Net Benefit (decision curve analysis) — measures clinical utility across possible intervention thresholds
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set (months 25–30, n₂). For TGNN: grid search over graph layer depth (1–4 layers), attention heads (4, 8), learning rate (1e-4, 1e-3, 1e-2), dropout (0.1, 0.3), and temporal encoding dimension (16, 32, 64). For logistic regression: grid search over C (inverse regularization strength: 0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10). Model selection uses AUROC on the validation set as the stopping criterion. Early stopping is not used; instead, all configurations are trained to convergence, and the model with highest validation AUROC is retained. The same validation set is used for both model types to ensure fair comparison. Test set (months 31–36) is never used during tuning or selection.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is reserved solely for final evaluation and is accessed exactly once at the end of the experiment. No hyperparameter tuning, feature engineering, or model selection occurs using test data. Final metrics (AUROC, sensitivity, PPV, calibration) are reported on the test set with 95% confidence intervals (via bootstrap resampling, 10,000 iterations). If performance metrics differ substantially between validation and test sets, data drift is investigated post-hoc but does not trigger re-tuning. A drift report is produced separately.
structured_choices.confound_controls: (1) Stratification by discharge reason during train/val/test split ensures case-mix balance across cohorts; (2) Socioeconomic status (insurance type and zip-code median income) and comorbidity burden are included as explicit features in both TGNN and baseline, allowing the models to learn their association with readmission; (3) Post-hoc stratified analysis: AUROC is computed separately for age groups (<65, 65+), insurance types, and comorbidity strata to check for heterogeneous treatment effects — if TGNN shows improvement in one stratum but not others, this flags potential confounding or population-specificity; (4) Subgroup analysis for discharge disposition (home, skilled nursing facility, etc.) — readmission risk differs by disposition; performance is evaluated within each disposition to confirm TGNN improvement is not an artifact of better prediction in a specific disposition; (5) No unmeasured confounds can be fully controlled in observational data, but the temporal structure and confound features reduce bias relative to static logistic regression. Limitations are clearly stated in the scope.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Temporal Graph Neural Networks for Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Source and Labeling

We would use 36 months of electronic health records from the regional hospital system. The dataset includes all inpatient discharge records with complete 30-day follow-up information. The outcome variable is binary: readmission within 30 calendar days of the index discharge (any admission for any reason qualifies). Labels are deterministic and locked at day 31 post-discharge; any patient with incomplete follow-up information is excluded from the analysis to prevent censoring bias.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

The data is divided chronologically: months 1–24 for training (approximately 60% of data), months 25–30 for validation (approximately 20%), and months 31–36 for test (approximately 20%). This temporal ordering is essential for two reasons. First, it prevents label leakage — the model cannot access future readmission outcomes when making predictions on past discharges. Second, it reflects real deployment conditions: hospital practices, patient populations, and clinical protocols drift over time, and a temporal split captures this drift. Within each split, we stratify by discharge reason category (medical, surgical, other) to ensure balanced case-mix across train, validation, and test sets. Stratification prevents distribution shift and ensures that readmission rate differences are not artifacts of cohort composition.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Raw signals from the EHR are: diagnosis codes (ICD-10), procedure codes (CPT), medications, lab results with timestamps, vital signs, and discharge disposition. All preprocessing transformers (scalers for continuous values, encoders for categorical codes, imputation strategies) are fit exclusively on the training set and then applied identically to validation and test sets.

Constructed features for the temporal graph include: static node features (age, sex, insurance type, Elixhauser comorbidity burden computed from diagnosis history), admission-level features (severity of illness score, length of stay), and temporal edge features (time intervals between prior admissions, treatment transitions, lab value trends over the prior 12 months). Critically, all temporal features are computed using only information available at discharge time — no post-discharge data is used.

Confound variables are explicitly included in both models: socioeconomic status proxies (insurance type, zip-code median income) and comorbidity count. This allows the models to learn the association between these variables and readmission rather than hiding confounding effects.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a Temporal Graph Neural Network (TGNN) using graph attention mechanisms with temporal encoding. The network represents each patient's clinical history as a dynamic graph: nodes are clinical events (admissions, labs, procedures), and edges encode temporal or causal relationships. The baseline is logistic regression trained on the same constructed feature set as the TGNN — but without explicit graph structure. The baseline receives summary statistics of temporal sequences (mean, max, min, standard deviation of lab values; count of prior admissions; time since last admission) rather than the full event graph.

Both models receive the same confound features and are tuned with equivalent effort. The TGNN undergoes grid search over architecture (graph depth, attention heads, dropout) and learning rate on the validation set. The baseline undergoes grid search over L2 regularization strength via cross-validation on the training set. This ensures that any performance difference reflects the value of temporal graph structure, not unequal tuning effort.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics and Validation Approach

The primary metric is AUROC (Area Under the ROC Curve). This metric is chosen because readmission prediction is fundamentally a ranking task: the hospital needs to identify high-risk patients for targeted intervention. AUROC is threshold-agnostic and stable across moderate class imbalances (readmission rates typically 10–25%), making it appropriate for operational decision-making. Secondary metrics include sensitivity at fixed specificity thresholds (0.85, 0.90), positive predictive value, Brier score for calibration, and decision curve analysis to measure clinical utility across intervention thresholds.

Hyperparameter selection uses the validation set (months 25–30) only. All model configurations are trained to convergence, and the model with the highest validation AUROC is selected. The test set is accessed exactly once at the end of the experiment for final metric reporting and is never used during tuning or selection.

### 6. Controls for Confounding and Scope

Confounds are controlled through: (1) stratified splits by discharge reason to balance case-mix; (2) explicit inclusion of socioeconomic and comorbidity features in both models; and (3) post-hoc stratified analysis by age group, insurance type, comorbidity burden, and discharge disposition to detect heterogeneous effects or population-specific biases.

The experiment establishes whether TGNN outperforms logistic regression on this dataset under these conditions. It does not establish causality or generalization to other hospitals without separate validation. Limitations due to unmeasured confounds (e.g., social support, adherence to follow-up care) are acknowledged. The temporal structure and explicit confound features reduce bias relative to static methods, but observational data cannot fully eliminate unmeasured confounding.
